paragraphs: This document is intended to provide guidelines that will help to unify and reinforce our brand identity and purpose across all lines of business, creating consistency through messaging and design.

No matter what business tier we're speaking to or channel we're speaking through, Chase should always be conscious of maintaining the core values that represent the entire brand. The subject matter and content may change from product to product or audience to audience, but our thinking and presentation should not.
Our audiences should always know that it is Chase that is speaking to them, and with the rules laid out herein, we can always be sure they do.

raw.headline: CHASE HELPS YOU MAKE THE MOST OF YOUR MONEY.

paragraphs: This is our brand purpose, a single articulation that captures both why and what we are. How the Chase brand behaves starts with a strong brand purpose. With every product we develop, every mention of our brand and every interaction we have with our customers, Chase is helping people with their financial decisions, no matter where they're starting or where they're trying to go.

Growth for the Chase brand is built on customer relationships. Developing those relationships means helping real people with real challenges. We know our customers see the world optimistically but live their lives realistically. Whatever life throws their way, they always find the best in it and go for it.

paragraphs: A strong brand purpose points to a clear set of brand principles. We find the extraordinary in the ordinary. We talk like you do. We are customer-serving, not self-serving. We keep things simple. These are the principles we live by.

raw.headline: MAKING THE MOST OF REAL LIFE IS BADASS.

long_text.paragraph_1: We're interested in real life, not lifestyles. We care about the real things that happen in the lives of real people, the good and the bad. We put their comprehension and experience at the forefront, showing them they're at the core of everything we do.
long_text.paragraph_2: They're the heroes, not us. We are supporters, advocates, innovators and inspirers-authenticaly human, empowering and trustworthy. We don't set their goals; instead, we help them build a path to their goals so the victory is theirs. We empower them to be badass, to triumph against the odds, and to make make the most of whatever comes their way.

raw.headline: REAL STORYTELLING
raw.quote: "Did I ever tell you the one about the..."

long_text.paragraph_1: The stories we choose to tell should reflect the lives our customers live. We want to be our customers' bank for a lifetime, for all the needs that fall into that lifetime. Life can be imperfect at times, so we want to make sure we acknowledge the tough moments as well as the ones meant for celebrating.
long_text.paragraph_2: The stories should be inspiring, showing how making the most of real life can be extraordinary. The more relatable, believable and authentic they are, the easier it will be for people to see how Chase can help them with their financial needs, in good moments and bad ones.

bullets.text: Show a situation that could believably happen in real life, and avoid cliché situations that are predictable or contrived.
Be written in an approachable, authentic and optimistic tone.
Integrate Chase products and services organically as a part of the story.

paragraphs: We want to reflect the relationship we have with our customers, so we talk to them like a real person, not a stuffy bank. We're approachable, relatable and genuine.

Our voice is knowledgeable, trustworthy, familiar, cohesive, conversational and straightforward. Our conversations are simple and clear, free of jargon and indisputably human. We focus on providing solutions and simplifying the complicated.

These traits form the foundation for every conversation we have with our customers, no matter who they are, no matter where or when they interact with us. Our consistent, unified brand and voice creates cohesion and comfort, and we introduce nuance to phrasing and vocabulary to reflect our relationship with the customer and the context of what they're doing.

content_should: Use an approachable tone that sounds like a conversation between good friends.
Sound knowledgeable and clear without sounding stuffy, robotic or condescending.

approved_example.headline:
Chase Pay. Get more.
At more places.
approved_example.body: Chase Pay® combines your cards, wallets, loyalty programs and rewards so you can carry less and get more at millions of places.

improper_usage_examples.text:
EARN POINTS ON YOUR PURCHASES WITH THE NEW CHASE PAY® MOBILE APP.
Visit chasepay.com to learn more about how you can apply your rewards points.

approved_example.headline: MAKE YOUR EVERYDAY PURCHASES. GET 1.5% CASH BACK. KICK YOUR FEET UP.
approved_example.body: With Chase Freedom Unlimited® you get 1.5% CASH BACK on every single purchase. Learn more at Chase.com/FreedomUnlimited.

improper_usage_examples.text:
EARN 1.5% CASH BACK ON EVERYDAY PURCHASES WITH THE CHASE FREEDOM UNLIMITED® CARD.
Visit Chase.com/FreedomUnlimited to find out how you can start earning 1.5% CASH BACK today.

long_text.paragraph_1: The way we talk about our products should be friendly and integrated comfortably into the copy. It should be conversational.
long_text.paragraph_2: With a consistent visual identity, headlines don't need to do all the heavy lifting of messaging. Subheads, imagery or color all add to brand recognition as well.

sections.guidelines_intro: The way we communicate about our products should:
bullets: 1. Be as free of industry jargon as we can make it.
2. Focus on the benefit to the customer, not just a product or feature itself.
3. Include product names naturally, rather than forcing them into dialogue in unnatural ways.
4. Reflect how someone would really talk, consistent with our JPMC voice.

long_text.paragraph_1: The way we talk about our products should be friendly and integrated comfortably into the copy. It should be conversational.

sections.guidelines_intro: The way we communicate about our products should:
bullets: 1. Be as free of industry jargon as we can make it.
2. Focus on the benefit to the customer, not just a product or feature itself.
3. Include product names naturally, rather than forcing them into dialogue in unnatural ways.
4. Reflect how someone would really talk, consistent with our JPMC voice.

sections.title: THE STAND-ALONE CHASE OCTAGON
text_blocks: The Chase Octagon is an important icon with a rich history. It provides our strongest brand recognition and should be treated with care, consideration and reservation. The Chase Octagon should never be used for ornamentation.
To continue building its equity, the Chase Octagon should have the most screen time in animations and the most prominence across static layouts.

The stand-alone Chase Octagon should only be used in circumstances that allow the Chase brand to be established elsewhere within a single communication. For example, within the Chase Mobile app, inside a retail branch or in other long- form communications consisting of multiple frames or pages.

is best used to take credit for our messaging. It
should be used as a formal sign-off to end the

raw.headline: Improper Usage of the Chase Logomark

long_text.lead: The Chase logomark should never be recreated, altered or separated in any way.
long_text.examples_note: The examples on this page demonstrate improper usage of the Chase logomark. These rules apply to all logomark variations.
long_text.caveat: Please note that these examples are not exhaustive, and best judgment should be used when applying the logomark in unique circumstances.

improper_usages.caption: Do not use the logomark below its minimum size.
Do not create new logomarks.
Do not use the Chase Octagon as a design element, such as a text bullet.
Do not add any effects, such as a drop shadow.
Do not use the Chase Octagon within typography.
Do not break the Chase Octagon apart or use it as a border.
Do not reverse the colors of approved logomarks.
Do not introduce new colors into the logomark.
Do not change proportions or shift the elements.
Do not stretch or skew the logomark.
Do not violate ADA compliance.
Do not fill the Chase Octagon with photography.
Do not crop the logomark.
Do not place the logomark on a shape or pattern.
Do not place the logomark over faces in photography.

infographic_elements.description: Grid of boxed visual examples showing improper logomark use. Each example contains the logomark variant in a framed box with a red X icon in the top-left corner to indicate incorrect usage.

right_column.headline: Brand Awareness
paragraphs: The Chase brand reaches across several tiers of business with a diverse array of sub-brands and partnerships. It is important to not create visual competition between these distinct identities in a single communication.
All collateral should ultimately feel like it is coming from one unified source—Chase—while still maintaining a level of differentiation between sub-brands and their corresponding audiences.

When sub-brands have the opportunity to represent themselves via the context of a product shot, headline, subheadline or image, they should rely less on their individual logomarks. All of our communications should eventually ladder up to the Chase masterbrand.

The diagram on this page shows how we should consider our logomarks with regard to the way we communicate. The number of these elements included in a layout will change based on how and where they are used, but the conceptual hierarchy shown here should not.

raw.headline: Improper Usage of Primary Product Logo Lockups

long_text.intended_usage: Primary product logo lockups are intended for use with either a single sub-brand wordmark or a stacked sub-brand logo lockup that includes the business tier.
long_text.page_summary: The examples on this page demonstrate improper usage of the primary product logo lockup. Please refer to the following guidelines to avoid improper usage:

figures.caption: Do not create horizontal logo lockups.
The Chase Octagon should not be placed to the right of a sub-brand logomark.
The Chase Octagon should not appear smaller than the sub-brand logomark.
Do not create logo lockups that include messaging beyond the product name and business tier.
Do not create logo lockups for services and programs that treat the Chase Octagon as the word "Chase" within a phrase.

bullets.text: Do not create horizontal logo lockups. The Chase Octagon may be placed next to a single sub-brand logo lockup or a stacked sub-brand logo lockup that includes the business tier.
Do not create logo lockups that include additional, tertiary messaging.
Do not use the Chase Octagon to replace the word "Chase" in logo lockups for services and programs that use it as part of a phrase.
The Chase Octagon should not appear smaller than the sub-brand logo lockup.
The Chase Octagon should not be placed to the right of the sub-brand logo lockup.

raw.headline: Proper Placement of Primary Product Logo Lockups

long_text.intro_paragraph: The examples on this page demonstrate proper and improper placement of the primary product logo lockup. Proper usage should minimize repetition and redundancies of logomark within a single communication.
long_text.instruction_lead: Please refer to the following rules for using primary product logo lockups:

bullets.text: The entire Chase logo lockup is used as a formal sign-off to end the communication.
The primary product logo lockup and the Chase logomark should not appear together within the same visible area.
When working with digital layouts, please ensure that only one lockup is visible within the same fold region.
If a sub-brand logomark is visible within a product shot, it does not need to be repeated. In this scenario, sub-brand names should be represented within headlines and body copy.

callouts.text: Do use the primary product logo lockup on one side of a double-sided print piece and the Chase logomark on the other.
Do use the primary product logo lockup at the start of a digital execution and the Chase logomark at the end.

headlines: Best Practices for Co-branded Logo Lockups

subsections.title: Best Practices for Co-branded Logo Lockups
subsections.content_verbatim: The examples on this page demonstrate best practices for minimizing brand confusion within a Chase-owned communication.

subsections.title: LOGOMARK PLACEMENT
subsections.content_verbatim: Chase branding should always appear first in the logo lockup.
details: In order to minimize brand confusion, Chase branding should always appear first in the logo lockup.
As a standard rule, the two logos are best presented side by side, with the Chase branding positioned to the left.
When horizontal space is extremely limited, it is okay to stack the logos. Chase branding should always be positioned at the top of the logo lockup.

subsections.title: STACKED CO-BRANDED LOGO LOCKUP TREATMENT
subsections.content_verbatim: (Intended for use when horizontal space is extremely limited)

subsections.title: THE DIVIDER LINE
subsections.content_verbatim: A thin divider line should always be used to separate logos within a co-branded logo lockup.

subsections.title: COLOR / CORRECT COLOR USAGE
subsections.content_verbatim: Logo colors should never be compromised in order to complement a partner logo.
examples_verbatim: Correct example shown: CHASE (in brand color) | amazon.com (partner logo) — with check indicator
Improper example shown: CHASE (modified/monochrome or altered) | amazon.com — with red X indicator

rules_and_guidelines_verbatim: Chase branding should always appear first in the logo lockup.
As a standard rule, the two logos are best presented side by side, with the Chase branding positioned to the left.
When horizontal space is extremely limited, it is okay to stack the logos. Chase branding should always be positioned at the top of the logo lockup.
A thin divider line should always be used to separate logos within a co-branded logo lockup.
Logo colors should never be compromised in order to complement a partner logo.

infographic_elements.description: CHASE logomark at left, thin vertical divider line, partner logomark (UBER) at right. Demonstrates side-by-side placement with Chase first.

infographic_elements.name: Stacked co-branded lockup example
infographic_elements.description: CHASE logomark positioned above partner logomark (UBER). Labeled as intended for use when horizontal space is extremely limited; Chase must be on top.

infographic_elements.name: Divider line visual
infographic_elements.description: Thin horizontal/vertical divider line used between logos to separate brands in lockup. Rule: A thin divider line should always be used.

infographic_elements.name: Correct color usage example
infographic_elements.description: Shows CHASE in its correct brand colors paired with partner brand (amazon.com) without altering Chase color — accompanied by a check icon to indicate correctness.

infographic_elements.name: Improper color usage example
infographic_elements.description: Shows CHASE with altered/compromised color (monochrome or modified) paired with partner brand (amazon.com) — accompanied by a red X to indicate incorrect usage.

global_guidance: Chase logomarks should always appear slightly larger than or equal to the partner logo.
No part of a co-branded logo lockup should ever be presented below its individual minimum size requirement. Please refer to relevant guidelines to ensure proper legibility and placement.

raw.headline: Improper Usage of Co-branded Logo Lockups

long_text.intro_paragraph: All co-branded logo lockups should maintain the proper constructs when presented within a Chase-branded communication. Rules regarding placement, scale and sub-brands should remain consistent across all executions.
long_text.guidance_lead_in: The examples on this page demonstrate improper usage of co-branded logo lockups. Please refer to the following guidelines to avoid improper usage:

bullets.text: Do not use the stand-alone Chase Octagon when creating a co-branded logo lockup.
Do not place a partner logo first within a co-branded logo lockup.
Do not use individual lockups that contain multiple layers of branding.
The partner logo should not overpower the Chase branding.
Do not create lockups for extended lines of business or internal teams.

long_text.brand_tagline_usage: Our brand tagline should live across the majority of our communications, aligning Chase's products and offerings with its brand purpose. The tagline is best used to close communications.
long_text.intro_line: Please refer to the following key rules for using the tagline:

golden_rules.text: The exact wording of the tagline should never be altered.
The tagline should only be presented as a lockup, never in live text or as part of a sentence.
The tagline should end every long-form, :30 and :15 film and radio spot.
All videos should conclude with the approved end frame and voiceover.
The tagline should always be voiced over by the voice of Chase, not other actors.
The tagline shouldn't be paired with additional logos, taglines, sub-brand messaging or lockups.
The tagline shouldn't be used where space is too tight or elements would be too cramped.

fonts.name: Arial

guidelines.headline_definition: Headlines are short, impactful statements. Their purpose is to capture attention and prompt the user to read more. They are best presented as simple, singular thoughts. Headlines should never be used to communicate detailed, explanatory messaging.
guidelines.running_headlines: Running headlines serve as navigational devices. They should establish the overarching topic in order to provide the reader with a greater context for the information being presented.
guidelines.introductory_text: Introductory text should be used as a bridge between headlines and body copy. Introductory copy should provide longer-form, more-detailed messaging. Introductory text may be used without a headline as long as a concept and/or category has previously been introduced.

In marketing communications (such as print, OOH or even email), sub-brand headlines should be treated consistently in all caps. Contextual headlines used in informational communications (such as websites or experiential) should always follow best practices for readability and may be treated in sentence or title case.

typography_texts: Recognized Chase sub-brands may use headline treatments unique to their individual branding.

In marketing communications (such as print, OOH or even email), sub-brand headlines should be treated consistently in all caps. Contextual headlines used in informational communications (such as websites or experiential) should always follow best practices for readability and may be treated in sentence or title case.
The sample headlines on this page show a variety of marketing communication text weights and brand colors. The leading and tracking should remain relatively consistent across sub-brands.

raw.headline: Brand Color Spectrum

long_text.para_1: This diagram shows the spectrum of visible colors as they relate to the Chase brand. The spectrum has been split into two halves-the top half showing colors that fall within Chase's brand color palette and the bottom half showing the colors that fall outside of it.
long_text.para_2: All the colors in the top half share the use of blue/cyan, aligning them with the Chase brand. Approved colors may be tinted lighter or darker but should never venture into the lower half.
long_text.para_3: Because Chase's brand equity relies upon its ownership of the color blue, opposing colors- yellow, orange, red and even purple-are considered off-brand and should only be used in special circumstances.
long_text.para_4: For more information regarding limited brand color use, please refer to pages 57-58.

long_text.paragraph_1: The diagram on this page represents the color hierarchy as it relates to Chase branding within layouts.
long_text.paragraph_2: Open white space creates a sense of sophistication, simplicity and order. raw.headline: Limited Brand Color Palette

paragraphs: The limited brand color palette takes its tones from the off-brand spectrum of color. Colors from this palette should only be used in extreme circumstances in which the brand palette becomes exhausted (e.g., a color-coded chart).
Restricted colors are only intended to provide additional differentiation and should never be used over our brand color palette.
For more information regarding restricted-color- palette usage, please refer to pages 51 and 58.

considerations.heading: Consider the following when using restricted colors:
items: 1. Off-brand colors should never be used for branding purposes.

raw.title: CTA Button Colors

long_text.overview: The examples on this page demonstrate suggested color treatments for styling call-to-action buttons. CTA colors are often dependent on the functionality of the site/placement in which they live.

primary.guidance: Primary CTA buttons should be the largest in scale and rendered in tints of blue.

headline_stats.headline: SAMPLE RANGES FOR CONTRAST COMPLIANCE
headline_stats.visual_callout: FAIL / PASS indicator associated with sample typographic contrast bands

paragraphs: All digital, non-video creative must adhere to Chase accessibility standards and pass Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.0 AA compliance.

Please test the point size and color of the text against the background color to ensure proper contrast levels are being used. To test contrast values, please visit paciellogroup.com/resources/ contrastanalyser/.

Listed below are some of the most common WCAG 2.0 AA considerations. Please note that this is not a complete list of all criteria to be met. The complete version of the guidelines can be found at w3.org/TR/WCAG20.

For any moving, blinking or scrolling information that starts automatically, lasts longer than five seconds and is presented in parallel with other content (e.g., carousels), there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop or hide it unless the movement is part of an activity in which it is essential.

bullets.text: Level AA requires a contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.
Large text is typically defined as 14 points or larger (typically 18.66 pixels) and bold.

Text or images of text that are part of an inactive user interface component, that are pure decoration or that are part of a picture that contains other significant visual content have no contrast requirement.
Text that is part of a logomark or brand name has no minimum contrast requirement.
Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action or prompting a response.
Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media, except when the media is a media alternative for text and is clearly labeled as such.

For any moving, blinking or scrolling information that starts automatically, lasts longer than five seconds and is presented in parallel with other content (e.g., carousels), there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop or hide it unless the movement is part of an activity in which it is essential.

subsections.title: Real-Life People
subsections.content_verbatim: Chase's brand DNA strives to celebrate the differences that make us all unique, as well as the quirky life moments we all experience. In turn, the subjects we shoot should feel like ordinary, everyday people. They should have imperfections and flaws. We should show them in scenarios that feel authentic, not contrived or staged.
subsections.additional_verbatim: When selecting photography with people, remember to consider diversity in terms of age, ethnicity, gender and lifestyle.

raw.headline: Interacting with the Camera

long_text.paragraph_1: Our photography should maintain a sense of candor and spontaneity. While it's okay for our subjects to be aware of the camera, in general they should not be posing for it unless the image is a portrait.
long_text.paragraph_2: Consider the various types of portraits: candid portrait (taken without the subject expecting or acknowledging the camera), environmental (shows the subject in their natural environment), classic portrait (headshot with the subject looking directly into camera) or lifestyle portrait (hybrid of candid/environmental, showing a snapshot or day in the life).
long_text.paragraph_3: If an image requires the person photographed to look directly into the camera, it should feel like we're capturing a candid moment.
long_text.paragraph_4: When we use a photograph featuring a person interacting with the camera, make sure:

portrait_types.definition: taken without the subject expecting or acknowledging the camera
shows the subject in their natural environment
headshot with the subject looking directly into camera
hybrid of candid/environmental, showing a snapshot or day in the life

The image does not feel staged, stiff or overly composed.

raw.headline: Making Our Celebrities Feel Approachable

The celebrity should be placed in an environment that is relatable to the audience.
The celebrity should be dressed in casual attire.

The imagery can't be confused for a different brand's product (such as athletic apparel or a sporting event).

paragraphs: Hero product photography generally leads communications from Chase, making it the first thing a customer will see and interact with. As such, it should feel relatable to the audience, grounding the product in the real world, where they would actually use it.

raw.headline: Selecting Hero Product Photography
intro_paragraphs: Strong hero product photography captures the products as they are in real life and is shot in a natural environment, using natural lighting and depth.
Please consider the following when selecting hero product photography:
bullets: 1. The product is placed in a natural setting that speaks to the offering.
2. The product does not look artificial or overly rendered.
3. The product isn't isolated on a solid-color background.
4. If shot at an angle, the product should not appear overly obstructed or distorted.
5. Partner logos should be visible on all product art.
captions: The product is placed in a natural setting that speaks to the offering.

usage_guidelines.heading: Usage of Secondary Product Art
paragraphs: Secondary product art may use isolated treatments that present our products outside of real-life photography. While the images are not captured in a photographic setting, they should still reflect some of our real-life principles.

paragraphs: Illustrations used in Chase communications should embody the principles we stand for. They should feel human, relatable and organic. They shouldn't be overly rendered or refined. Stylistically, illustrations should feel editorial. They can be witty or sophisticated but never cartoonish or whimsical.

Effective use of illustration allows us to present complex concepts, narratives or situations in a single visual that is infused with our brand identity. Illustrations can be more expository than photography and should be used to support messaging that may be geared toward educating the audience.

raw.headline: Illustration Usage

sidebar_paragraphs: Illustrations can often do things photographs and icons can't, clarifying complex information and making it more digestible. They are most useful as a means of assimilating multiple ideas into a single visual and can unify them visually through a consistent, recognizable brand filter.

Illustrations should not be gratuitously paired with content in order to give it pizzazz. Their purpose isn't for ornamentation or to overstate the obvious. Illustrations should be used to simplify situations and distill ideas down to their very core.
long_text.top_figure_caption: Use illustration to present ideas that cannot be captured in a single photograph. Illustration is best used to make the complex clearer, uniting a variety of images and categories through a single brand filter.
long_text.bottom_figure_caption: Illustrations should never be used to gratuitously dress up content. They are intended to enhance the clarity of complex ideas and not to convolute or diminish simple communications.

side_explanations: Use illustration to present ideas that cannot be captured in a single photograph. Illustration is best used to make the complex clearer, uniting a variety of images and categories through a single brand filter.
Illustrations should never be used to gratuitously dress up content. They are intended to enhance the clarity of complex ideas and not to convolute or diminish simple communications.

Illustrations can often do things photographs and icons can't, clarifying complex information and making it more digestible. They are most useful as a means of assimilating multiple ideas into a single visual and can unify them visually through a consistent, recognizable brand filter.

Illustrations should not be gratuitously paired with content in order to give it pizzazz. Their purpose isn't for ornamentation or to overstate the obvious. Illustrations should be used to simplify situations and distill ideas down to their very core.

raw.headline: How to Choose an Illustration

long_text.narrative_1: The right kind of illustration can feel smart, earnest and approachable. It should be infused with just as much humanity as a photograph. A variety of mediums, such as ink drawings, water colors or collages, may be used to match specific content.
long_text.narrative_2: Chase sub-brands can have distinct illustration styles as long as each design approach remains consistent and ladders up to Chase's brand principles.
long_text.caption_top: Illustrations should feel simple, with a human touch. They should not look overly rendered or 3-D. Avoid whimsical, childlike or cartoonish visuals. Our illustration style should feel smart, witty and sophisticated.
long_text.caption_middle: Illustrations should always use artwork that represents real life. Avoid using abstract artwork or patterns to fill in backgrounds.
long_text.caption_bottom: Illustrated characters should be realistic, identifiable and anatomically correct. The human figure should not be exaggerated for dramatic effect.

checklist: There is a human touch to it.
The rendering feels handcrafted and imperfect.
It feels effortless and suggests ease of use.
It is simple and approachable.
It incorporates the Chase color palette.
Illustrated characters are realistic, identifiable and anatomically correct.
The imagery is representative, not abstract.
The rendering style feels editorial, smart, witty and sophisticated.

raw.headline: Incorporating Textures and Collages
raw.instruction_lead: When using textures or collage elements within an illustration, please adhere to the following:

long_text.paragraph_1: Textures can help add a human touch and ground an illustration in the real world. Presenting an illustration on a surface such as a blackboard or notebook can reinforce the concept that the information was worked out by a real person.
long_text.paragraph_2: Weathering treatments can make illustrations that would otherwise be cold and impersonal feel more organic and handmade. They should not, however, make them feel rustic or grungy.
long_text.paragraph_3: Using photography as a collage element (such as a wood texture to represent a desk or an image of a mobile device to reinforce a real-life product) can help ground the illustration in reality.

bullets.text: The texture makes the work feel organic and handmade.
It is used subtly and doesn't make the work feel old or grungy.
It conceptually connects the illustration style to the content.
It represents a real-world product.

paragraphs: Secondary illustrations should serve as a follow-up to hero visuals. They are best used to emphasize a product benefit, service or call to action.

Stylistically, secondary illustrations should serve as a bridge between our hero illustration style and our iconography. They should carry more detail than an icon but should also still be legible at a medium-to- small scale.
Visually, secondary illustrations should be rendered in a monoline style that makes use of a limited palette with minimal color fills. In digital spaces, they should be containable and versatile.

raw.caption: Secondary illustrations should be rendered in a monoline style.

5. The illustration uses a restrained color palette with minimal color fills.

paragraphs: Iconography is a visual language intended to enhance navigation and differentiation. Icons should help the user absorb and process information quickly. In digital spaces, they should always be treated as clickable elements.

Icons are best used to reference a category, explain an action or provide clarification. They should be universally readable, instantly recognizable and clear as to their intent. They should not be open to interpretation.

If the icon doesn't say anything, it doesn't mean anything. If the icons are attempting to present too many ideas at once, they're trying to say too much. In such a scenario, please consider using a larger-scale illustration to express a more complex concept. Icons are meant to telegraph specific meaning or classification, not complex concepts.
All icons must be approved prior to use. Any changes or additions to the icon set will require a development release.

In such a scenario, please consider using a larger-scale illustration to express a more complex concept. Icons are meant to telegraph specific meaning or classification, not complex concepts.
All icons must be approved prior to use. Any changes or additions to the icon set will require a development release.

raw.headline: Icon Design

long_text.Icon Design: The best icons are extremely simple in design and say more with less. Remember, an icon is not worth a thousand words—as a visual language, it's worth one.

subsections.title: RENDERING STYLE
subsections.text: Chase's icons are rendered in a simple, monoline style with interior negative space. Only the smallest icons should be filled with solid color. The stroke weight and level of detail should remain consistent across all icon sets.

subsections.title: SCALABILITY
subsections.text: Our icons will need to be rendered at both medium and small scales to support a variety of spaces. Medium-sized icons can carry slightly more detail than their small-scale counterparts. Ensure that an icon can be simplified and scaled down without losing its core messaging.

subsections.title: MODIFIERS
subsections.text: Icons representing similar content should use a simple modification device to help distinguish individual features.

subsections.title: LABELING
subsections.text: Icons should be combined with labels explaining their content. These labels offer reinforcement for users as they learn the visual language.

raw.headline: Proper Usage of Icons

long_text.intro_paragraph: The effectiveness of icons as a visual language is dependent upon the way they're used. When used properly, they can provide a stronger aesthetic and enhance the user experience. Improper use of icons will cause them to lose their impact and dilute messaging.
long_text.instruction_line: Please refer to the following list to determine proper usage of icons:

bullets: 1. The icon is approved for use.
2. The icon is being used to represent a single category, action or indication.
3. The icon should not be used to define a multilayered concept or an overly specific category.

4. The meaning and intent of the icon
are easily understood and universally
discernible.
5. The design is visually appealing.
6. The icon is simple enough to be legible at an extremely small size.
7. The content difference is noticeable and the icon is easily distinguished from others within the library.

8. The icon should always maintain a
functional relevance.

long_text.intro: The way we present data needs to be clear, consistent and visually simple and should reinforce the Chase brand's look and feel.
long_text.guidance_lead: The following should be considered with regard to creating charts and graphs:

bullets: 1. Refrain from using gradients or drop shadows. All charts and graphs should use solid blocks of color.

subsections.title: Color Usage for Data Visualization

long_text.paragraph_1: All Chase communications should lead with our brand color palette (blues, greens and gray scale).
long_text.paragraph_2: In extreme circumstances in which the color palette becomes exhausted (e.g., a color-coded chart), it is acceptable to use additional limited colors, provided they're used sparingly.
long_text.intro_to_list: When selecting additional colors for data visualizations, please consider the following:

bullets_verbatim: 1. The design should only include restricted colors if absolutely necessary for differentiation.
2. Limited colors should be assigned to information furthest from Chase's brand purpose and objectives (tracking competitors, error messages, negative numbers, etc.).
3. Frames and borders should be used to separate restricted colors from the Chase brand color palette.

subsections.title: Animation Styles

paragraphs: The animation styles we use should be closely tied to our core brand principles. They should never be overly exaggerated. They should be clean and considered.

We want every animation to accurately reflect Chase as a smart, trustworthy financial institution. Movements and transitions should be smooth, subtle and smart. It's important that the animation style is not distracting. It should always be used to support our message through clear visualization.

subsections.criteria_intro: Our animation styles should satisfy the following criteria:

criteria.text: The animation is not distracting or overly exaggerated.
The transitions should be simple, smooth and elegant.
The movement is appropriate with regard to the content.
Avoid using movements that feel bouncy, abrupt or cartoonish.

subsections.title: Animation Styles

paragraphs: The animation styles we use should be closely tied to our core brand principles. They should never be overly exaggerated. They should be clean and considered.

We want every animation to accurately reflect Chase as a smart, trustworthy financial institution. Movements and transitions should be smooth, subtle and smart. It's important that the animation style is not distracting. It should always be used to support our message through clear visualization.

subsections.criteria_intro: Our animation styles should satisfy the following criteria:

criteria.text: The animation is not distracting or overly exaggerated.
The transitions should be simple, smooth and elegant.
The movement is appropriate with regard to the content.
Avoid using movements that feel bouncy, abrupt or cartoonish.

subsections.reference: For more on motion graphics and animation, please refer to the document titled Motion Guidelines.

long_text.para_1: Video supers should reflect our typography guidelines on pages 44-49. They should be simple in content, clean in design and legible against footage. Be careful not to overload a single frame with too much information.
long_text.para_2: It's important that every piece of storytelling, whether super or voiceover, ladders up to the final delivery of our brand purpose. While each line of business (and respective type of film) may have its own specific style, the tagline should be consistently voiced over by the voice of Chase.

Please refer to the following rules for
additional guidance:

bullets.text: Super treatments should be simple in content, clean in design and legible against footage.
Do not present a large amount of information within a single frame.

Text transitions should adhere to our
animation guidelines.
Approved end-frame treatments should not be altered or substituted.
Videos should conclude with the approved tagline layout and voiceover track.
Additional supers and acquisitions should not follow or diminish the brand tagline.
The approved animation style should be used as much as possible.
All end frames should be accompanied by the approved voiceover track.
